perturbation.original_text: 20.2 Governing Law. This Agreement and any dispute arising from the performance or breach hereof shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of California, without reference to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text:
20.13 Language.
20.2 Governing Law. This Agreement and any dispute arising from the performance or breach hereof shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of Japan, without reference to conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The contract originally specified California law as the governing law but now it specifies the laws of Japan. This is a legal contradiction because it misrepresents the applicable legal framework for disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts established choice of law principles, particularly concerning contracts. Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws outlines how courts determine which jurisdiction's law applies, typically considering the parties' intent and the jurisdiction with the most significant relationship to the transaction. By placing the governing law clause within the 'Language' section and switching it to Japanese law while the company HQ is in California, its enforceability becomes questionable, thus creating uncertainty and potential legal disputes regarding which jurisdiction's laws should prevail.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The contract modification directly contradicts the original agreement by changing the governing law from California to Japan. The 'conflict of laws' snippet indicates that when jurisdictions differ, a court determines which law to apply. By explicitly changing the governing law, the modified contract introduces a structural flaw by misrepresenting the applicable legal framework for disputes.

perturbation.original_text: 17.1 Insurance. Each party shall secure and maintain in effect during the term of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years thereafter insurance policy(ies) underwritten by a reputable insurance company and in a form and having limits standard and customary for entities in the biopharmaceutical industry for exposures related to the Lead Compounds. Such insurance shall include general liability, clinical trial liability and products liability coverage with respect to such party's performance of the Development Program and commercialization of Lead Compounds hereunder. Upon request by the other party hereto, certificates of insurance evidencing the coverage required above shall be provided to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text:
20.13 Language.
17.1 Insurance. Each party shall secure and maintain in effect during the term of this Agreement and for a period of one (1) year thereafter insurance policy(ies) underwritten by a disreputable insurance company and in a form and having limits standard and not customary for entities in the biopharmaceutical industry for exposures not related to the Lead Compounds. Such insurance shall include general liability, clinical trial liability and products liability coverage with respect to such party's non-performance of the Development Program and non-commercialization of Lead Compounds hereunder. Upon request by the other party hereto, certificates of insurance evidencing the coverage required above will not be provided to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The modification moves the insurance clause to a section unrelated to risk management and changes key terms, such as reducing the insurance period, specifying 'disreputable' insurers, excluding coverage for Lead Compounds, and stating that insurance evidence will not be provided. This directly undermines the purpose of insurance, creating a legal contradiction and potentially violating standard business practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By significantly weakening the insurance requirements and moving them to an irrelevant section (Language), the modification introduces a contradiction violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. This covenant, implied in every California contract under California Commercial Code § 1201, requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of the contract. Altering the insurance clause in this way suggests an intent to undermine the protection the agreement was meant to provide, contradicting the spirit of cooperation and fair dealing.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessi
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces multiple changes that contradict standard legal and business practices related to insurance. The original text requires insurance from a reputable company for exposures related to Lead Compounds, but the changed text specifies a 'disreputable' insurer and excludes coverage for Lead Compounds. This undermines the purpose of insurance, which is to provide reliable financial protection against risks. Additionally, the change specifies that certificates of insurance will not be provided, removing a standard verification mechanism. The Law Explanation suggests that this contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, indicating an intent to undermine the agreement's intended protection. The relocation of the clause to a section on 'Language' further demonstrates a structural flaw that obscures the importance of the insurance requirement. Snippet 1 defines 'agreement' broadly but doesn't directly address insurance requirements, so its relevance is limited. However, combined with the other factors, the modifications create a legal contradiction by directly undermining the intended insurance coverage.

perturbation.original_text: 10.5 Records; Inspections. Astellas shall keep, and require its Affiliates and Sublicensees to keep, complete, true and accurate books of accounts and records for the purpose of determining payments due pursuant to this Agreement. Such books and records shall be kept for at least [ * ] following the end of the calendar quarter to which they pertain. FG shall keep, and require its Sublicensee(s) to keep, complete, true and accurate books of accounts and records for the purpose of verifying the accuracy of the [ * ] Percentage and Fully Burdened Costs. Such records will be open for inspection at the principal place of business of each party (the "Inspected Party") during such [ * ] period by an independent auditor chosen by the other party (the "Inspecting Party") and reasonably acceptable to the Inspected Party for the purpose of verifying the amounts payable by Astellas to FG hereunder or the accuracy of the [ * ] Percentage and/or Fully Burdened Costs. Such inspections may be made no more than once each calendar year, at reasonable times and on reasonable notice. Any books of accounts or records shall not be inspected more than once. The independent auditor retained by the Inspecting Party shall be obligated to execute a reasonable confidentiality agreement with the Inspected Party prior to commencing any such inspection, which, among other customary clauses, contains the provisions to the effect that such auditor shall not disclose to the Inspecting Party any information other than as necessary to accomplish the purpose of the inspection. Inspections conducted under this Section 10.5 shall be at the expense of the Inspecting Party. Any underpaid or overcharged amounts that are discovered will be paid by the Inspected Party, and with interest on such underpaid or overcharged amounts at the rate set forth in Section 10.2 above. The parties will endeavor to minimize disruption of the Inspected Party's normal business activities to the extent reasonably practicable.
perturbation.changed_text:
20.13 Language.
10.5 Records; Inspections. Astellas shall keep, and require its Affiliates and Sublicensees to keep, books of accounts and records that are not complete, true and accurate for no purpose related to determining payments due pursuant to this Agreement. Such books and records will not be kept for any amount of time following the end of any period. FG shall not keep, and not require its Sublicensee(s) to keep, books of accounts and records for the purpose of verifying the accuracy of anything. Such records will not be open for inspection at the principal place of business of each party (the "Inspected Party") during any period by anyone and not by an independent auditor chosen by the other party (the "Inspecting Party") and not reasonably acceptable to the Inspected Party for the purpose of verifying the amounts payable by Astellas to FG hereunder or the accuracy of anything. Such inspections may be made many times each calendar year, at unreasonable times and on no reasonable notice. Any books of accounts or records can be inspected as many times as someone wants. The independent auditor retained by the Inspecting Party will not be obligated to execute a reasonable confidentiality agreement with the Inspected Party prior to commencing any such inspection, which, among other clauses, contains the provisions to the effect that such auditor shall disclose to the Inspecting Party any information other than as necessary to accomplish the purpose of the inspection. Inspections conducted under this Section 10.5 shall be at the expense of the Inspected Party. Any underpaid or overcharged amounts that are discovered will not be paid by the Inspected Party, and with no interest on such underpaid or overcharged amounts. The parties will not endeavor to minimize disruption of the Inspected Party's normal business activities to the extent reasonably practicable.
perturbation.explanation: The modification moves the audit and records clause to the 'Language' section, making it appear as a linguistic quirk rather than a critical financial control. Furthermore, the entire substance of the section is twisted to now allow for financial misconduct. This contradicts the duty to maintain accurate financial records.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this agreement isn't directly subject to SOX, the principles are applicable due to Fibrogen's status as a publicly traded company. By eliminating the need for accurate records, allowing inspections at any time without notice and removing confidentiality, the modification fundamentally undermines financial transparency and accountability, contradicting the core principles of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (specifically Section 404, codified as 15 U.S. Code § 7262), which requires companies to establish and maintain internal controls to ensure accurate financial reporting. Making this change would violate the requirement for 'complete, true, and accurate books of accounts and records' for the purpose of determining payments, and violates financial best practices.

Management assessment of internal controls (a) Rules required The Commission shall prescribe rules requiring each annual report required by section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title to contain an internal control report, which shall- (1) state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control stru
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the spirit and intent of maintaining accurate financial records for determining payments and verifying financial data. The original text mandated 'complete, true and accurate books of accounts and records,' while the changed text explicitly states that the books of accounts and records 'are not complete, true and accurate for no purpose related to determining payments due pursuant to this Agreement.' The Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX), specifically Section 404, codified as 15 U.S. Code § 7262, requires companies to establish and maintain internal controls to ensure accurate financial reporting. The provided snippet from 15 USC 7262 focuses on 'Management assessment of internal controls' and the requirement for annual reports to contain an internal control report, stating 'the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control structure.' By allowing for inaccurate records and restricting inspections, the modified contract undermines financial transparency and violates the principles of SOX.
